title: Exterminating and Pest Control Services

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561710 ($8,715,666,468) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561710 ($19,563,962,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
First-Line Supervisors of Housekeeping and Janitorial Workers
Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
Pesticide Handlers, Sprayers, and Applicators, Vegetation
First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $139,825 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561710); class <5: 8,882 firms, avg revenue/firm $236,662 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $37,038 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 2,491 firms, avg revenue/firm $736,951 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $115,333 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,373 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,587,316 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $248,415 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 766 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,665,939 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $730,219 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 70 firms, avg revenue/firm $21,941,586 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,433,858 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $396,989,381 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $62,128,838 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 70
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.rationale: State-licensed applicators physically treating structures on recurring routes; mature AI does not perform the visit. Small automatable sliver: service documentation, on-site pest ID from photos, report write-ups. Route optimization augments but does not remove the technician.
Scheduling, dispatch, billing, renewal processing - the genuinely automatable core; AI phone/scheduling agents and FSM-embedded automation apply directly.
Multi-branch reporting, pricing and routing analytics automate; local hiring, technician retention and commercial account relationships persist as human work.
Lead handling, quoting and follow-up automate; inspection-based selling (termite/WDO reports) and door-to-door residential sales remain human.
Route supervision and training are field/relationship work; AI widens span of control modestly via dashboards.
Inbound-call handling, rescheduling, renewal calls - AI voice agents already deployed in the industry for exactly this.
General admin, correspondence, document handling - core LLM-automatable clerical work.
Physical application work; not automatable by current AI beyond documentation sliver.
Supervises the shrinking clerical pool; workload tracks the automatable clerical base but retains exception-handling and people management.
AR/AP, reconciliation, payment posting - among the most automatable functions with current AI plus existing accounting software.
Mixed tail (HR, marketing, drivers, misc field/office); weighted toward field/physical given industry composition, with a modest automatable office fraction.

pi_dist.rationale: Pricing is recurring per-visit/quarterly/annual plans set locally with no payer, auditor or fee schedule above the operator - efficiency gains are not contractually handed back to customers (unlike cost-plus or audited billing). The operator owns the recurring residential account (brand/phone number attaches and transfers in acquisitions - the reason the industry rolls up) and commercial accounts sit under contract with compliance documentation. Deducted: Google Local Services lead auctions and national ad-budget brands (Rollins/Orkin, Terminix, Aptive door-to-door) increasingly intermediate residential customer acquisition, competing away part of the spread on new-customer pricing; commercial food/healthcare accounts have procurement leverage. No mechanism forces AI savings to be passed through on existing recurring plans, so capture is above the midpoint.

pi_moat.rationale: State applicator licensing constrains labor supply and blocks casual entry; physical presence and route density are real local barriers (the same AI scheduling gain is worth far more inside a dense route); recurring contracts create switching inertia. Threat side: generic AI cannot bypass the firm - the customer needs a licensed technician to physically treat the structure; DIY-plus-AI diagnosis nibbles only at the residential low end; smart-trap/remote monitoring can thin commercial visit frequency but is typically sold by the operator itself. Barriers are meaningful but not fortress-grade: licensure is attainable, and local operators lack proprietary data.

t50_years.derivation: Current material adoption ~15% (PCT poll Aug 2024: 14% use AI extensively; FieldRoutes 2026 report: any-use 35%, up from 19% a year earlier - a near-doubling in one year). Historical analog: standalone field-service-management software took roughly two decades to reach majority penetration (PestPac founded 1986, cloud FSM ~2012 onward), but AI is arriving pre-embedded in the FSM platforms (FieldRoutes/ServiceTitan, PestPac) the industry already uses, so diffusion should run much faster than the standalone-software analog. Interpolating: material adoption from ~15% to 50% at the current trajectory (roughly +8-10pp/yr of material use as any-use converts to material) gives ~4 years. Chain: 15% + 4yr x ~9pp/yr = ~50%.

current_adoption_pct.source: PCT (Pest Control Technology) reader poll; FieldRoutes 2026 State of the Trades report
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: PCT poll (published 2024-08-23): '36 percent are occasionally using AI' and '14 percent use it extensively'. FieldRoutes (https://www.fieldroutes.com/blog/ai-adoption-in-pest-control): AI use among pest control operators 'rose from 19% to 35%' year over year, used for 'marketing automation, route optimization and scheduling... call transcription and summaries'. Material = extensive/regular AI use in the automatable office/scheduling/marketing functions, not occasional experimentation; anchored on the 14% extensive-use figure, nudged up for the steep trajectory since the poll.

historical_analogs.value: Field-service-management/routing software: ~20 years from availability to majority adoption (PestPac founded 1986; cloud FSM PestRoutes/FieldRoutes from ~2012; PestPac serves ~5,000+ customers and claims the majority of the PCT Top 100, while the long tail of ~13,600 firms adopted far more slowly). Cloud FSM reached broad small-operator adoption in roughly 10 years.
historical_analogs.source: Field Service Guide PestPac review; Aplos AI FieldRoutes vs PestPac comparison
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: 'PestPac is a field service management (FSM) platform founded in 1986'; 'PestPac serves approximately 5,000+ customers... the company claims to serve the majority of the top 100 pest control operators' (https://aplosai.com/blog/fieldroutes-vs-pestpac). No single time-series of FSM penetration exists; the ~10-20 year range is inferred from founding dates vs current penetration.

owners_60plus_pct.source: Teamshares silver-tsunami research (economy-wide small-business proxy; no pest-control-specific owner-age survey found)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: '40% of small business owners are Baby Boomers' (born 1946-1964, i.e. aged ~62-80 in 2026). Applied to pest control as a proxy: about two-thirds of pest control businesses are single-location, family-run operators (Briostack industry statistics), so the economy-wide small-business owner age profile is a reasonable stand-in. ESTIMATE basis: no industry-specific owner-age figure located.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: NPMA PestWorld magazine (Nov/Dec 2023, 'Generational Legacy'): only ~34% of family businesses have a robust, documented succession plan; trade press (mypmp.net succession series; The Pest Control Broker) documents family owners reaching retirement and selling to competitors/PE for lack of internal successors; economy-wide 'over 70% [of small businesses put up for sale] fail to find a buyer' (Teamshares).

active_consolidators.source: CT Acquisitions 2026 Pest Control PE Roll-Up Tracker
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: '22 active US pest control PE platforms' including Rollins, Rentokil-Terminix, Anticimex (EQT), Aptive, Certus (Imperial Capital + LMI; 7 disclosed acquisitions in 2024, most active US acquirer), Hawx, Rockit, PestCo, AXN Growth Partners (Shore Capital), Barefoot (Incline Equity; 5 disclosed 2024 acquisitions). Corroborated by Capstone Partners Pest Control Sector Update (Jan 2025).

buy_mult.source: CT Acquisitions Pest Control Business Valuation Guide 2026
buy_mult.figure_quoted: '$1M-$5M EBITDA: 5.5x to 6.5x for businesses with 70-85% recurring revenue; 6.5x to 8.0x for those with 85-95% recurring revenue'; most transactions in the $500K-$5M EBITDA range land between 5x and 8x. Median single-location/small-firm deal taken at 6.0x. Basis check per rule 3: the same guide states 'Above $1M in EBITDA, buyers use EBITDA' (sub-$500K deals are SDE-quoted and were not used).

exit_mult.source: CT Acquisitions pest control exit guide / valuation guide 2026
exit_mult.figure_quoted: 'Platform-quality pest control businesses trade at 12x to 18x EBITDA in 2026, with Rentokil paying 19.9x EBITDA for Terminix in the largest disclosed transaction on record.' Valuation guide: consolidators buy independents at 7-9x and roll into platforms valued at 12-17x+ at platform level. Low end of the 12-18x platform range taken as the conservative $10M+ EBITDA exit comp.

pricing_structure.value: Predominantly recurring subscription/contract billing set locally by the operator: residential quarterly/monthly plans (~85% of residential revenue is recurring), commercial accounts under annual service contracts with compliance documentation, plus one-time/termite jobs (WDO inspections, fumigation, initial treatments) as the non-recurring balance. No hourly billing, no cost-plus, no third-party payer or fee schedule.
pricing_structure.source: CT Acquisitions valuation guide and PE demand explainer
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: '85.4% of residential pest control revenue is recurring, vs 20% to 30% for HVAC and 10% to 20% for plumbing'; recurring-revenue share is the primary multiple driver (85%+ recurring crosses the platform threshold).

C.arithmetic: C = 10 x pi_dist x pi_moat = 10 x 0.65 x 0.60 = 3.9

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw of 0.0909435 implies up to ~9.09pp of revenue freed as EBITDA uplift at full capture - well above the 3pp floor, so no inconsistency; A = 2.5 (< 5) reflects a measured 4-year t50, not a value shortage. The check's trigger condition (uplift < 3pp while A >= 5) does not fire.

terminal_value.justification: Pests are eliminated by a state-licensed technician physically treating the structure - mature AI does not perform the visit, so the paid product survives. The automatable surface is the ~30-35% of wage bill in office, scheduling, sales, supervision and management around the route (our ai_replaceable_share 0.191 sits consistent with that once technician labor is excluded). Kept separate per the prompt: (a) AI cutting operator overhead - real and bounded, captured in V; (b) mechanisms removing the paid visit - largely absent: DIY-plus-AI touches only the residential low end, and smart-trap remote monitoring thins commercial visit frequency but is sold by the operator itself and shows no measured revenue-per-account erosion in the sources reviewed. Edge pressure exists on capture (Google LSA auctions and national ad brands intermediating residential acquisition), which is priced into pi_dist = 0.65, not into terminal class. Demand for the truck roll survives mature AI: DURABLE.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Confirmed. V was built solely from labor_share (QCEW/ECEC/SUSB) x role-level automatable fractions grounded in task content (licensed physical work vs clerical/scheduling/bookkeeping); A was derived from PCT/FieldRoutes adoption surveys and the FSM-software diffusion analog. Neither V nor A cites deal activity, consolidator counts, or multiples. Consolidation evidence (22 platforms, Certus/Barefoot deal counts, 12-18x platform multiples) was used only in B (CFD) and M, per rule 2.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: recurring locally-set subscription plans (~85% of residential revenue recurring), annual commercial contracts, plus one-time termite/WDO work. No cost-plus, hourly, audited or fee-schedule billing exists that would contractually hand AI savings back to clients, so deflation pressure is competitive rather than structural: Google Local Services auctions and national brands can compete away part of the AI spread on new residential customer acquisition, and commercial procurement can rebid. This is reflected in pi_dist = 0.65 (rather than 0.8+ for fully captive pricing) with the mechanism stated; pi_moat = 0.60 reflects licensure/route-density barriers net of the modest bypass threats.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A = min(10, 10/t50): t50 = 3 gives A = 3.333, t50 = 6 gives A = 1.667; S moves roughly (A_new/2.5)^0.2, i.e. from ~3.58 (t50=6) to ~4.01 (t50=3) - about +/-0.2 on S.
Economy-wide Boomer-owner proxy, not industry-specific. At 0.30: OW = 0.5714+0.1 = 0.6714, B = 4.621, S ~ 3.66; at 0.50 OW clamps to 0.9+0.1 = 1.0, B = 5.640, S ~ 3.81. Range ~ +/-0.13 on S.

M = 4x(exit-6)/6: exit 9 gives M = 2.0, exit 15 gives M = 6.0; S ranges ~3.30 to ~4.11. The single most score-sensitive researched input; 12x is the low end of a broker-quoted 12-18x platform range, but disclosed roll-up entry deals cite 7-10x, so the arbitrage width is genuinely uncertain.

reviewer_note: Formula outputs reported as computed; no overrides. Key caveats: (1) n_band = 70 is a floor - the provided chain's generic 15.65% margin excludes the 20-99 employee class (766 firms, avg revenue $4.7M), but well-run pest operators commonly run 20%+ EBITDA, at which part of that class enters the $1-10M band; TD (and hence B) is understated if so (n_band = 400 would put TD at 0.65 and B at ~6.55). Used as provided per rule 6. (2) owners_60plus_pct is an economy-wide small-business proxy (40% Boomer-owned) - no pest-control-specific owner-age survey was found; the succession-shortage flag rests on NPMA/trade-press documentation of retirement-driven sales rather than a quantified successor gap. (3) buy_mult/exit_mult come from broker/M&A-advisory content (CT Acquisitions, corroborated by Capstone and Potomac commentary), which has a sell-side bias toward wide arbitrage; exit_mult was taken at the low end (12x) to compensate, and sub-$500K SDE-quoted deals were excluded per rule 3. Termite retreat/repair guarantee liabilities require QoE reserves that quoted multiples may not reflect. (4) current_adoption_pct 0.15 interprets 'material' as extensive/regular use (PCT: 14% extensive) rather than any-use (35%); if material adoption is already ~25%, t50 shortens toward 3 years. (5) Formula-vs-intuition: none material - the mid-pack S with strong B/M and weak A matches the qualitative picture of a heavily consolidated, licensure-protected route business where AI cuts overhead but not the truck roll.
